---
title: "Arrowfeather Threeawn (Aristida purpurascens)"
slug: "aristida-purpurascens"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/180851585/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Arrowfeather Threeawn"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Liliopsida"
tax_order: "Poales"
tax_family: "Poaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Liliopsida", "Poales", "Poaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/180851585/medium.jpg" alt="Arrowfeather Threeawn" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/aclarke2">
aclarke2</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/107519613">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Liliopsida
-   **Order:** Poales
-   **Family:** Poaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=158667)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Rare species in Florida found in dry sandy or rocky soils, pine flatwoods, and disturbed areas.

## Natural history notes

A perennial grass characterized by its narrow leaves and distinctive three-branched awns that give the seed heads a feathery appearance. In Florida, it is found in dry, sandy habitats such as sandhills, scrub, and open pine woodlands. It grows primarily during the warmer months and is well adapted to fire-maintained ecosystems.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/239361-Aristida-purpurascens-tenuispica)
-   [Local profile](https://florida.plantatlas.usf.edu/plant/species/832)
